system_prompt:
You are extracting the structural outline of a guitar method book from its front matter.

The user will give you the verbatim text of the first ~20 pages of a book.
Identify the chapters or major sections of the book (whichever the book uses
— some method books use "Lesson", "Chapter", "Part", "Section", "Unit").

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "chapters": [
      { "n": 1, "title": "...", "start_page": <int>, "end_page": <int> },
      ...
  ] }

Rules:
- `n` is the 1-indexed chapter number you assign (independent of the book's
  own numbering — book may start at 0 or use Roman numerals).
- `title` is the chapter's title as it appears in the book.
- `start_page` is the first page of the chapter; `end_page` is the last.
- If the front matter doesn't list page numbers, infer from the chapter
  headings you see — start_page is where the chapter HEADING appears, and
  end_page is one less than the next chapter's start_page (or the book's
  last page for the final chapter).
- Skip the table of contents itself, the title page, the copyright page,
  the author bio — they are not "chapters" in this sense.
- If the book is short and not subdivided, output a single chapter covering
  the entire content.

                          COMPLETE CHORD MELODY




Welcome to Complete Chord Melody by Jazz Guitar Online, it’s great to have you here!

The material in this course will set you up for success as a chord melody guitarist.

The key to sounding convincing with chord melody is to have solid arranging techniques under your
belt. This course will give you a clear, step-by-step method to creating your own arrangements that
sound great and are easy on the hands.

This course is best followed on the online version, with the ability to watch video, bookmark pages,
and ask questions.

Click here to access the online version of this eBook.


Course Overview

Here is a quick overview of what you will learn in this course.



Module 1 – Chord Melody Quick Start

In this module, you’re going to:

   Discover a simple 4-step process for arranging chord melodies on guitar.
   Get your ears excited with classic chord melody renditions by master players.
   Look at all the options for chord melody right-hand techniques and choose the one that suits your
   playing style best.



Module 2 – Chord Vocabulary

In this module, you’re going to:

   Explore Joe Pass’s concept of ‘The Three Harmonic Families’ – and how knowing this can greatly
   simplify arranging chord melodies.
   Acquire a useful ‘pallet’ of chords to build your chord vocabulary and make arranging as easy as
   ‘painting by numbers’.
   Learn how to understand chord labels.
   How to find colorful voicings from a single shape.



Module 3 – Let’s Create a Chord Melody Arrangement!

In this module, you’re going to:

   Go into more detail in the specific steps for arranging playable and great-sounding chord melodies.
   Learn how to analyze the harmony of a progression and intervals in the melody for your chosen
   jazz standard.
   How to apply great-sounding voicings to key points in your arrangement to bring it to life.
   A variety of example chord melody arrangements that sound great in a trio.


Module 4 – Arranging For Solo Guitar

In this module, you’re going to:

   Get a comprehensive training on arranging for solo jazz guitar.
   Discover the most useful voicings to use for solo chord melodies.
   A step-by-step walkthrough of creating a solo guitar arrangement from scratch.
   Spice up your solo guitar arrangement with a selection of handy arranging tricks and techniques
   such as walking bass, octaves, passing chords, and more.
   Example solo guitar arrangements demonstrating how to apply the arranging techniques in action.



Module 5 – More Advanced Arranging Techniques

In this module, you’re going to:

   Discover a variety of chord substitution techniques to take your chord melody arrangements to the
   next level – backcycling, tritone substitution, melodic common tone subs, and more.
   How to incorporate jazz lines and phrases into your arrangements to get more spontaneity and life
   in your performances.
   Tying everything together and practicing your arrangement to make it ready for the concert stage.



Appendix 1 – Chord Dictionary Reference

This Appendix features the complete collection of chord voicings covered throughout the course for
easy reference.



Appendix 2 – Fingerstyle Guitar Exercises

This appendix provides a variety of useful drills and etudes for developing fingerstyle techniques for
playing chord melody arrangements.




 About the Audio in this eBook: If you are connected to the internet while working with this eBook,
 all you have to do is click the link of each audio example to play it. If you are not connected, you
 will have to download the audio files here and manually open them by double clicking the .mp3
 files.


Listening Exercises - Great Chord Melody Renditions

To kick things off, check out the following Youtube videos that feature a variety of classic recordings
by master players in a chord melody style.

Listening is an important part of any jazz student’s study, so make sure you don’t skip this step.




JOE PASS – SATIN DOLL LIVE




We’ll kick things off with the man who pretty much defined the classic approach to solo jazz chord
melody, Joe Pass.

He’s my biggest influence when it comes to chord melody style. Simply put, every bar Joe plays in
this performance is a jazz education in itself.


BARNEY KESSEL – AUTUMN LEAVES




An evocative rendition of Autumn Leaves by the great Barney Kessell.

This rendition starts with an unaccompanied solo guitar version before moving into a chord melody he
plays along with the band (which I call a ‘trio’ chord melody throughout this course) – a good example
of these 2 approaches in the one performance.


ED BICKERT – DO NOTHING ‘TILL YOU HEAR FROM ME




If I was only allowed to choose one player to emulate in a trio setting it would be Ed Bickert – laid
back yet refined, I could listen to him all day long.

As Bickert plays a solo he often interchanges between single line, chord stabs, and full chord soloing,
skilfully creating interest and variety as you can hear in the video above.

What I love most about Bickert’s playing is his unique approach to chord voicings: easy on the hands,
but very sophisticated.


TED GREENE – SEND IN THE CLOWNS




Ted Greene was relatively unknown to the general public outside of jazz guitar circles, but his
contribution to the understanding of the guitar is profound.

His one and only album, Solo Guitar (1977) is considered by many to be one of the most important
solo jazz guitar albums ever to be recorded. This is one of the tracks from that groundbreaking
recording.

This performance features lush chord voicings on a 7-string guitar, with captivating texture and
delicate ornaments such as harp harmonics.


LENNY BREAU – ALL BLUES




If I had to pick one player out of the pantheon of jazz guitarists who was the most creative,
spontaneous, and dynamic, the clear choice for me would be Lenny Breau.

This performance of All Blues from the classic album Live at Bourbon Street features Lenny Breau’s
“2-note chord” approach which uses bare-bones voicings in the accompaniment.

Inspired by pianists, this innovative technique enabled him to execute complex melodies and rhythmic
approaches over the chords, making it sound like 2 guitars are playing at once!


TAL FARLOW – MISTY




Nicknamed “The Octopus” because of the way his large, quick hands spread over the fretboard, Tal
Farlow’s dazzling chord melody abilities are showcased in this performance of Misty.

This recording is important to include as here’s an example of solo chord melody played entirely with
a plectrum rather than fingerstyle.


GEORGE VAN EPS – KISSES




George Van Eps had a jazz guitar career that spanned 60 years and was still performing into his 80s.

In the 1930s he invented a model of guitar adding an extra bass string to a standard six-string
instrument. This new 7-string guitar enabled him to play low basslines in his arrangements and is a
defining feature of his sound.


Module 1 - Chord
Melody Quickstart


How to Arrange Chord Melodies – The Basic in a Nutshell

Here’s a ‘bird’s eye overview’ on how to arrange a chord melody.

We’ll be going into much more detail later in the course, but in this section, you will learn what you
need to do in a nutshell for creating your own chord melody arrangements:

   Pick a suitable tune
   Change the key if necessary for better range and playability
   Learn the melody on top 2 (or 3) strings
   Choose chord voicings to add below melody notes


Step 1 - Picking a Suitable Tune

Not all tunes are playable as chord melodies or can be difficult.

For your first arrangement, I recommend you stick to ballads, rather than the faster bebop tunes. The
slower tempo and longer-held melody notes in ballads will be much easier for your first project.

I’m going to use Autumn Leaves for this example as it ticks all these boxes – it can be played as a
ballad and it has some long melody notes, a perfect choice.




   Play audio example 1-1


Step 2 - Change Key if Necessary for Range and Playability

On guitar, finding a suitable range for your arrangement is important:

   If the arrangement is too high, it will sound like it doesn’t have enough depth.
   On the other hand, if your arrangement is too low in the register, it will sound too dark and
   muddy.

Finding the ideal range is usually what will dictate your choice of key. You’ll need to experiment to
find a key that works.

A good rule of thumb:


 Search for a key where you can play the melody on the top 3 strings, roughly anywhere from the
 3rd to the 9th fret.


Some of those notes of Autumn Leaves in Gm are too low – notice in the previous example how many
notes are on the 4th and 5th strings.

So instead, let’s try the other key Autumn Leaves is commonly played in – E minor:




   Play audio example 1-2


Step 3 - Learn the Melody on the Top 2 (or 3) Strings

This is where you need to experiment a bit. We need to be able to play the melody on the top 2
strings (maybe dipping into the 3rd string occasionally) and around the 3rd to 9th fret ideally.

Let’s now change the TAB of the melody line so that it mainly sits on the top 2 strings.

This will mean you will need to play more horizontally over the fretboard, rather than staying in a
single position. Watch the video below to notice how the fingering has been adapted with this in mind.
